title: Lost in Translation
wikitext:
[]: Lost in Translation             Quote Post by darth_mark » Fri Feb 03, 2006 3:01 pm it's hard enough to figure out evangelion without wading through dubs and subs in english/engrish. it makes me wonder if people in Japan have already discussed the whole thing to death and finally figured it out. are there Japanese equivalents of evamonkey, tines and/or mdwigs? maybe they're laughing at us gaijin still trying to make sense of it after all these years.?

[]:             Quote Post by poetic_assassin » Fri Feb 03, 2006 3:39 pm This show would be pretty old to them about now though, wouldn't it? Even when it was new for us it was -what?- a year old to them?

[]: Re: Lost in Translation             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Fri Feb 03, 2006 4:19 pm darth_mark wrote: are there Japanese equivalents of evamonkey, Shin-seiki, Reichu and/or mdwigs? maybe they're laughing at us gaijin still trying to make sense of it after all these years.?

[]:             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Fri Feb 03, 2006 4:21 pm I've often wondered this. What exactly has the Japanese fan community come up with. How much have they debated. We should really try and use translation resources, paticularly automated ones, to try an get a feel for Eva-analysis in Japan. Does anyone know any good forums to start looking on?

[]:             Quote Post by Faußtin » Sat Feb 04, 2006 6:34 am Ummm... Theres a lil' problem. Many of us - including myself - don't speak Japanese, so if you find some good stuff, please be so kind and translate it, okay? :roll:

[]:             Quote Post by The Green Automobile » Sun Feb 05, 2006 9:04 am From what I've heard/seen/read/worked out with extremely shoddy Japanese skills, there was a cottage industry of criticism and theory around Evangelion in Japan, but it's far less vociferous today than in its heyday in the 90's. (Though apparently people did get seriously into it; Gainax-bunnies ought to remember that in FLCL, Kamon Nandaba went mad after writing "a whole book on the deep mysteries of Eva.") I have no direct knowledge of the state of the fan community, but I'm working on a mini-thesis on anime as we spea--...type, and in my scholarly travels I've come across a couple of "proper" articles in Japanese on Evangelion; not in fanzines though, I mean proper smarty-pants journals and stuff. Natch, these wouldn't really give much of a feel for the Japanese fandom, but if I can find them again and find someway to translate 'em all nicely, it might be kinda vaguely cool. Nevertheless, even this long after the series, I don't think even the Japanese will have totally grokked Evangelion yet. It's the Ulysses of anime, people will be forever wondering what exactly it all meant. :3

[]:             Quote Post by darth_mark » Mon Feb 06, 2006 9:03 am The Green Automobile wrote: but if I can find them again and find someway to translate 'em all nicely, it might be kinda vaguely cool.

Article: Lost in Translation
 a cottage industry of criticism and theory around Evangelion in Japan, but it's far less vociferous today than in its heyday in the 90's. (Though apparently people did get seriously into it; Gainax-bunnies ought to remember that in FLCL, Kamon Nandaba went mad after writing "a whole book on the deep mysteries of Eva.") I have no direct knowledge of the state of the fan community, but I'm working on a mini-thesis on anime as we spea--...type, and in my scholarly travels I've come across a couple of "proper" articles in Japanese on Evangelion; not in fanzines though, I mean proper smarty-pants journals and stuff. Natch, these wouldn't really give much of a feel for the Japanese fandom, but if I can find them again and find someway to translate 'em all nicely, it might be kinda vaguely cool. Nevertheless, even this long after the series, I don't think even the Japanese will have totally grokked Evangelion yet. It's the Ulysses of anime, people will be forever wondering what exactly it all meant. :3
